output_text:
Restructured the section for conventional intro flow, flagged underspecified model details, and identified verification gaps in the empirical claims.

## Diagnosis

**Structural problem.** The five paragraphs read like an abstract, a results teaser, a motivation, a related-work paragraph, and a model spec — concatenated. The current order (contributions → results → motivation → related work → formalism) is unusual and forces the reader to accept the method before learning why it matters. For an intro section, the conventional order is motivation → gap → contribution (with a one-line results preview) → roadmap. The formal model belongs in §2 (Method), not the intro.

**Underspecified model.** The generative description has gaps that a reviewer will catch:
1. `p_LLM(·|j)` is "estimated from a held-out auto-generated reference set" — but you don't say how (kernel density? empirical categorical? a fine-tuned scorer?), and the reference set isn't characterized (which LLMs? sampled how?). This is the load-bearing piece of the model.
2. The annotator's "own confusion matrix" has no prior specified — Dirichlet rows are standard, but say so.
3. `Gamma(2, 0.5)` is ambiguous between rate and scale parameterizations; NumPyro uses concentration/rate, so make it explicit (and these priors put substantial mass on extreme Beta shapes — worth justifying or sensitivity-checking).
4. `σ_w = 0.21` is introduced without definition; presumably the posterior std of `θ_i` across workers, but say so.

**Empirical claims that need backing or qualification.**
- "30–46% of MTurk text-production submissions contain LLM output" — cite. If this is Veselovsky et al. (2023), name them; the range suggests you're collapsing multiple studies and the reader deserves to see which.
- OPEN-CROWD-2024 and AmazonMTurk-Annotated — I cannot verify these exist; if they're your construction, say so; if external, cite.
- Results are reported for 2 of 6 tasks. Why? If the other 4 showed null or negative effects, the abstract is cherry-picking. Either report all six (table) or say "the two tasks with sufficient gold coverage."
- The "improvement over majority vote" needs a baseline comparison to existing annotator-quality models (Dawid-Skene, MACE) — otherwise the contribution is unclear.

**Related-work gap.** The claim that no prior model "explicitly accommodates a contaminating generative process distinct from the annotator's own judgment" is too strong. Spammer-detection models (Raykar & Yu 2012; Hovy et al. 2013's MACE) explicitly model a "not-trying" component that's distinct from the worker's judgment. Your novelty is that the contaminant has *known structure* (the LLM distribution), not that contamination is modeled at all. Reframe accordingly — it's a stronger and more defensible claim.

## Suggested revision

> **§1 Introduction**
>
> The widespread availability of instruction-tuned LLMs has changed crowdsourcing: recent audits estimate that 30–46% of MTurk text-production submissions contain LLM-generated content [cite]. Because such crowdsourced text is, in turn, used to train and evaluate the next generation of language models, undetected contamination closes a feedback loop that quietly inflates apparent model quality and corrupts evaluation [cite].
>
> Existing tools for handling unreliable annotators — Dawid–Skene confusion matrices [cite], item-response models [cite], and spammer-detection variants such as MACE [cite] — treat low-quality responses as noise or as draws from an uninformative "not-trying" distribution. None exploit the fact that LLM-generated responses follow a *known, structured* distribution that can be estimated independently. Modeling this distribution explicitly should yield sharper estimates of which annotations to discount, and from which workers.
>
> We present **CrowdMix**, a hierarchical Bayesian mixture model that jointly infers (i) a per-annotator LLM-use propensity and (ii) the underlying gold-label distribution, treating each annotation as a mixture of an annotator's own (confusion-matrix–distributed) judgment and a shared LLM response distribution estimated from auto-generated references. Implemented in NumPyro and fit with NUTS, the model returns posterior contamination estimates at task and worker granularity, which downstream consumers can use to reweight or filter annotations.
>
> On the AmazonMTurk-Annotated subset of OPEN-CROWD-2024 (12,440 annotations, 6 text-production tasks), CrowdMix recovers a posterior mean LLM-use rate of 0.34 (95% HDI [0.29, 0.39]) with marked between-worker heterogeneity (posterior SD of per-worker θ_i = 0.21). On the two tasks with held-out gold coverage, CrowdMix-reweighted majority vote improves F1 by 4.7 points (paraphrase) and 3.1 points (summarization) over plain majority vote, and by 2.1 / 1.4 points over Dawid–Skene aggregation. [Note: add the other 4 tasks or justify their exclusion.]

Then move the formal generative description to the opening of §2, expand it with the priors on the confusion matrix rows, and add a paragraph specifying how `p_LLM(·|j)` is estimated.

## Uncertainty disclosure
- I cannot verify the existence or contents of "OPEN-CROWD-2024" or "AmazonMTurk-Annotated"; the citation suggestions for the 30–46% figure (Veselovsky et al.) and for MACE (Hovy et al. 2013) are from memory and should be confirmed.
- My claim that MACE/Raykar models a "not-trying" component distinct from worker judgment is correct in spirit but the exact framing varies by paper — double-check before adopting that wording.
- The baseline comparison to Dawid–Skene I inserted in the suggested revision is a placeholder; only keep it if you actually ran it.
